story:
Wanda Wilbur watched Sheriff R.T. Barnes strap on his grip-worn .38 and sweat-dark belt. "You goin' out? You been patrolin' near every afternoon. Who my s'posed to talk to?" He gave her a grin. "Missoura's now the meth capital accordin' to the Feds. Gotta keep the hopheads outa our backyard." She clicked her tongue and tucked a bobby pin. "Lucky for us, we got six-term Sheriff R.T. Barnes on the trail." He waved her off. "I'm gonna go cruise the river. The wife calls, tell her I'll be home by six."

Butler County didn't have a town over two hundred, so the Black River was where everything happened. The cruiser rattled across the old wooden bridge, the sheriff scanning the lazy shimmer of water and the trees clinging to its banks. He pulled up near the path to Big Eddy, where kids swam and showed off on hot afternoons. Laughter and the slap of the rope swing echoed as he came down the trail.

Nobody noticed him until he was standing in the sun with them, girls stretched on towels and boys in their tighty whiteys yodeling like Tarzan. "Tommy McFadden! Get over here!" Tommy trudged over. "By my count, you're the only one here of drinkin' age," the sheriff said, eyes drifting to the coolers. "Those two chests could hold 'bout two cases a beer. By law, I gotta arrest underage drinkers and anyone buys liquor for 'em. So. I'm gonna sit and smoke. When I'm back on duty, if I see any full cans that ain't been dumped in that river, y'all gonna be on river detail." They knew he meant trash duty more than jail, and they hated it more. When he ground out his cigarette and came back, the coolers were sloshing empty and the lectures were over.

Back in the car, he rolled slow with the radio low. A flicker near a bend caught his eye. He stopped, eased through poison ivy and sticker bushes toward the water. At the bank, he recognized the man crouched there. "Eh, Jody. What brings you out here?" Jody kept his hands steady, holding a burlap feed sack just at the surface. The sack moved. "Aw, got me a litter a cats, Sheriff. Damn critters go feral." The sheriff looked uneasy. "Jesus, Jody—" "It's God's way. Ya rather I shot 'em?" He looked away. "How's Mama and Betty?" "Mama's good. Betty's eight months. First one. Hormone rolly coaster." The sheriff seized the out. "Maybe I'll go check on 'em." Jody didn't turn. "That ain't necessary. You know I don't like strangers at my place." "I'm no stranger," the sheriff said, hustling back to the cruiser.

He took the tunnel road to the house. Three pit mixes flowed around the bumper, silent and watching. Mama came out, shooed them off, and let him in. Her hands twisted. "Things been kinda hard with Betty." "You take her to the Doc? Or a hospital?" "No. She's here, in the back." He headed for the only closed door. "Sheriff! No. She's—she's come up with a miscarriage and don't feel like seeing no one." He slowed, then turned the knob anyway.

Betty lay on a narrow bed under a thin gray sheet. Her belly still showed the swollen curve of recent pregnancy, soft and loose under the worn fabric. He took off his hat. "How you handling things, Betty?" She stared at the floor. "Bad. Real bad." "In what way?" "I lost my baby girl. Guess she was stillborn." He frowned. "Really. Jody never said that. I just saw him down to Black River, drownin' a litter a cats." Silence pressed the walls. Betty didn't lift her eyes. "Sheriff Barnes," she said softly, "we don't have no cats."
